<AVAILABLE_TOOLS>
Code Keyword Search(*args, **kwargs) - Performs keyword search on container source code for exact text matches. Input should be a function name, class name, or code pattern. Use this first before semantic search tools for precise lookups.
CVE Web Search(*args, **kwargs) - Searches the web for information about CVEs, vulnerabilities, libraries, and security advisories not available in the container.
Call Chain Analyzer(*args, **kwargs) - Checks if a function from a package is reachable from application code through the call chain.
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name'.
    Example 1: 'urllib,parse'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.function_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: (is_reachable: bool, call_hierarchy_path: list).
Function Caller Finder(*args, **kwargs) - Finds functions in a package that call a specific library function. GO ecosystem only.
    Input format: 'package_name,library.function(args_with_literals)'. 
    Example: 'github.com/namespace/package_name,errors.New("text_literal'")'.
    Returns: ['package,caller1', 'package,caller2'] or [].
Function Locator(*args, **kwargs) - Mandatory first step for code path analysis. Validates package names, locates functions using fuzzy matching, and provides ecosystem type (GO/Python/Java/JavaScript/C/C++).
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name' or 'package_name,class_name.method_name'.

    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: {'ecosystem': str, 'package_msg': str, 'result': [function_names]}.

Your role is to methodically answer investigation questions using available tools to determine if vulnerabilities are exploitable in the specific container context. You have access to the container's source code, documentation, and dependency information through specialized search and analysis tools.

<TASK>
Answer the investigation question using the available tools. If the input is not a question, formulate it into a question first. A Tool Selection Strategy is provided to help you decide which tools to use. Focus on answering the question. Include your intermediate reasoning in the final answer.
</TASK>

<AVAILABLE_TOOLS>
Code Keyword Search(*args, **kwargs) - Performs keyword search on container source code for exact text matches. Input should be a function name, class name, or code pattern. Use this first before semantic search tools for precise lookups.
CVE Web Search(*args, **kwargs) - Searches the web for information about CVEs, vulnerabilities, libraries, and security advisories not available in the container.
Call Chain Analyzer(*args, **kwargs) - Checks if a function from a package is reachable from application code through the call chain.
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name'.
    Example 1: 'urllib,parse'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.function_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: (is_reachable: bool, call_hierarchy_path: list).
Function Caller Finder(*args, **kwargs) - Finds functions in a package that call a specific library function. GO ecosystem only.
    Input format: 'package_name,library.function(args_with_literals)'. 
    Example: 'github.com/namespace/package_name,errors.New("text_literal'")'.
    Returns: ['package,caller1', 'package,caller2'] or [].
Function Locator(*args, **kwargs) - Mandatory first step for code path analysis. Validates package names, locates functions using fuzzy matching, and provides ecosystem type (GO/Python/Java/JavaScript/C/C++).
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name' or 'package_name,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 1: 'libxml2,xmlParseDocument'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: {'ecosystem': str, 'package_msg': str, 'result': [function_names]}.
</AVAILABLE_TOOLS>

<TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>
Code Keyword Search: Exact text matching for function names, class names, or imports
Call Chain Analyzer: Checks if functions are reachable from application code
Function Caller Finder: Finds which functions call specific library functions
Use 'Function Caller Finder' + 'Call Chain Analyzer' together to trace function reachability
CVE Web Search: External vulnerability information lookup
</TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>

[{"content": "You are an expert security analyst investigating Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) in container images. Your role is to methodically answer investigation questions using available tools to determine if vulnerabilities are exploitable in the specific container context. You have access to the container's source code, documentation, and dependency information through specialized search and analysis tools.

<TASK>
Answer the investigation question using the available tools. If the input is not a question, formulate it into a question first. A Tool Selection Strategy is provided to help you decide which tools to use. Focus on answering the question. Include your intermediate reasoning in the final answer.
</TASK>

<AVAILABLE_TOOLS>
Code Keyword Search(*args, **kwargs) - Performs keyword search on container source code for exact text matches. Input should be a function name, class name, or code pattern. Use this first before semantic search tools for precise lookups.
CVE Web Search(*args, **kwargs) - Searches the web for information about CVEs, vulnerabilities, libraries, and security advisories not available in the container.
Call Chain Analyzer(*args, **kwargs) - Checks if a function from a package is reachable from application code through the call chain.
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name'.
    Example 1: 'urllib,parse'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.function_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: (is_reachable: bool, call_hierarchy_path: list).
Function Caller Finder(*args, **kwargs) - Finds functions in a package that call a specific library function. GO ecosystem only.
    Input format: 'package_name,library.function(args_with_literals)'. 
    Example: 'github.com/namespace/package_name,errors.New("text_literal'")'.
    Returns: ['package,caller1', 'package,caller2'] or [].
Function Locator(*args, **kwargs) - Mandatory first step for code path analysis. Validates package names, locates functions using fuzzy matching, and provides ecosystem type (GO/Python/Java/JavaScript/C/C++).
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name' or 'package_name,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 1: 'libxml2,xmlParseDocument'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: {'ecosystem': str, 'package_msg': str, 'result': [function_names]}.
</AVAILABLE_TOOLS>

<TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>
Code Keyword Search: Exact text matching for function names, class names, or imports
Call Chain Analyzer: Checks if functions are reachable from application code
Function Caller Finder: Finds which functions call specific library functions
Use 'Function Caller Finder' + 'Call Chain Analyzer' together to trace function reachability
CVE Web Search: External vulnerability information lookup
</TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>

<REACT_FORMAT>
Follow this format exactly (start each line with one of the specified prefixes):

Question: the input question you must answer
Thought: think about what action to take next
Action: the tool to use, must be one of [Code Keyword Search, CVE Web Search, Call Chain Analyzer, Function Caller Finder, Function Locator]
Action Input: the specific input for that tool
Observation: the result returned by the tool
... (repeat Thought/Action/Action Input/Observation cycle as many times as needed)
Thought: I now know the final answer
Final Answer: provide the answer with supporting evidence from your investigation
</REACT_FORMAT>

    Input format: 'package_name,library.function(args_with_literals)'. 
    Example: 'github.com/namespace/package_name,errors.New("text_literal'")'.
    Returns: ['package,caller1', 'package,caller2'] or [].

input.value.stringValue:
[{"content": "You are an expert security analyst investigating Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) in container images. Your role is to methodically answer investigation questions using available tools to determine if vulnerabilities are exploitable in the specific container context. You have access to the container's source code, documentation, and dependency information through specialized search and analysis tools.

<TASK>
Answer the investigation question using the available tools. If the input is not a question, formulate it into a question first. A Tool Selection Strategy is provided to help you decide which tools to use. Focus on answering the question. Include your intermediate reasoning in the final answer.
</TASK>

<AVAILABLE_TOOLS>
Code Keyword Search(*args, **kwargs) - Performs keyword search on container source code for exact text matches. Input should be a function name, class name, or code pattern. Use this first before semantic search tools for precise lookups.
CVE Web Search(*args, **kwargs) - Searches the web for information about CVEs, vulnerabilities, libraries, and security advisories not available in the container.
Call Chain Analyzer(*args, **kwargs) - Checks if a function from a package is reachable from application code through the call chain.
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name'.
    Example 1: 'urllib,parse'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.function_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: (is_reachable: bool, call_hierarchy_path: list).
Function Caller Finder(*args, **kwargs) - Finds functions in a package that call a specific library function. GO ecosystem only.
    Input format: 'package_name,library.function(args_with_literals)'. 
    Example: 'github.com/namespace/package_name,errors.New("text_literal'")'.
    Returns: ['package,caller1', 'package,caller2'] or [].
Function Locator(*args, **kwargs) - Mandatory first step for code path analysis. Validates package names, locates functions using fuzzy matching, and provides ecosystem type (GO/Python/Java/JavaScript/C/C++).
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name' or 'package_name,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 1: 'libxml2,xmlParseDocument'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: {'ecosystem': str, 'package_msg': str, 'result': [function_names]}.
</AVAILABLE_TOOLS>

<TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>
Code Keyword Search: Exact text matching for function names, class names, or imports
Call Chain Analyzer: Checks if functions are reachable from application code
Function Caller Finder: Finds which functions call specific library functions
Use 'Function Caller Finder' + 'Call Chain Analyzer' together to trace function reachability
CVE Web Search: External vulnerability information lookup
</TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>

<REACT_FORMAT>
Follow this format exactly (start each line with one of the specified prefixes):

Question: the input question you must answer
Thought: think about what action to take next
Action: the tool to use, must be one of [Code Keyword Search, CVE Web Search, Call Chain Analyzer, Function Caller Finder, Function Locator]
Action Input: the specific input for that tool
Observation: the result returned by the tool
... (repeat Thought/Action/Action Input/Observation cycle as many times as needed)
Thought: I now know the final answer
Final Answer: provide the answer with supporting evidence from your investigation
</REACT_FORMAT>

[{"content": "You are an expert security analyst investigating Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) in container images. Your role is to methodically answer investigation questions using available tools to determine if vulnerabilities are exploitable in the specific container context. You have access to the container's source code, documentation, and dependency information through specialized search and analysis tools.

<TASK>
Answer the investigation question using the available tools. If the input is not a question, formulate it into a question first. A Tool Selection Strategy is provided to help you decide which tools to use. Focus on answering the question. Include your intermediate reasoning in the final answer.
</TASK>

<AVAILABLE_TOOLS>
Code Keyword Search(*args, **kwargs) - Performs keyword search on container source code for exact text matches. Input should be a function name, class name, or code pattern. Use this first before semantic search tools for precise lookups.
CVE Web Search(*args, **kwargs) - Searches the web for information about CVEs, vulnerabilities, libraries, and security advisories not available in the container.
Call Chain Analyzer(*args, **kwargs) - Checks if a function from a package is reachable from application code through the call chain.
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name'.
    Example 1: 'urllib,parse'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.function_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: (is_reachable: bool, call_hierarchy_path: list).
Function Caller Finder(*args, **kwargs) - Finds functions in a package that call a specific library function. GO ecosystem only.
    Input format: 'package_name,library.function(args_with_literals)'. 
    Example: 'github.com/namespace/package_name,errors.New("text_literal'")'.
    Returns: ['package,caller1', 'package,caller2'] or [].
Function Locator(*args, **kwargs) - Mandatory first step for code path analysis. Validates package names, locates functions using fuzzy matching, and provides ecosystem type (GO/Python/Java/JavaScript/C/C++).
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name' or 'package_name,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 1: 'libxml2,xmlParseDocument'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: {'ecosystem': str, 'package_msg': str, 'result': [function_names]}.
</AVAILABLE_TOOLS>

<TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>
Code Keyword Search: Exact text matching for function names, class names, or imports
Call Chain Analyzer: Checks if functions are reachable from application code
Function Caller Finder: Finds which functions call specific library functions
Use 'Function Caller Finder' + 'Call Chain Analyzer' together to trace function reachability
CVE Web Search: External vulnerability information lookup
</TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>

<REACT_FORMAT>
Follow this format exactly (start each line with one of the specified prefixes):

Question: the input question you must answer
Thought: think about what action to take next
Action: the tool to use, must be one of [Code Keyword Search, CVE Web Search, Call Chain Analyzer, Function Caller Finder, Function Locator]
Action Input: the specific input for that tool
Observation: the result returned by the tool
... (repeat Thought/Action/Action Input/Observation cycle as many times as needed)
Thought: I now know the final answer
Final Answer: provide the answer with supporting evidence from your investigation
</REACT_FORMAT>

[{"content": "You are an expert security analyst investigating Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) in container images. Your role is to methodically answer investigation questions using available tools to determine if vulnerabilities are exploitable in the specific container context. You have access to the container's source code, documentation, and dependency information through specialized search and analysis tools.

<TASK>
Answer the investigation question using the available tools. If the input is not a question, formulate it into a question first. A Tool Selection Strategy is provided to help you decide which tools to use. Focus on answering the question. Include your intermediate reasoning in the final answer.
</TASK>

<AVAILABLE_TOOLS>
Code Keyword Search(*args, **kwargs) - Performs keyword search on container source code for exact text matches. Input should be a function name, class name, or code pattern. Use this first before semantic search tools for precise lookups.
CVE Web Search(*args, **kwargs) - Searches the web for information about CVEs, vulnerabilities, libraries, and security advisories not available in the container.
Call Chain Analyzer(*args, **kwargs) - Checks if a function from a package is reachable from application code through the call chain.
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name'.
    Example 1: 'urllib,parse'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.function_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: (is_reachable: bool, call_hierarchy_path: list).
Function Caller Finder(*args, **kwargs) - Finds functions in a package that call a specific library function. GO ecosystem only.
    Input format: 'package_name,library.function(args_with_literals)'. 
    Example: 'github.com/namespace/package_name,errors.New("text_literal'")'.
    Returns: ['package,caller1', 'package,caller2'] or [].
Function Locator(*args, **kwargs) - Mandatory first step for code path analysis. Validates package names, locates functions using fuzzy matching, and provides ecosystem type (GO/Python/Java/JavaScript/C/C++).
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name' or 'package_name,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 1: 'libxml2,xmlParseDocument'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: {'ecosystem': str, 'package_msg': str, 'result': [function_names]}.
</AVAILABLE_TOOLS>

<TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>
Code Keyword Search: Exact text matching for function names, class names, or imports
Call Chain Analyzer: Checks if functions are reachable from application code
Function Caller Finder: Finds which functions call specific library functions
Use 'Function Caller Finder' + 'Call Chain Analyzer' together to trace function reachability
CVE Web Search: External vulnerability information lookup
</TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>

<REACT_FORMAT>
Follow this format exactly (start each line with one of the specified prefixes):

Question: the input question you must answer
Thought: think about what action to take next
Action: the tool to use, must be one of [Code Keyword Search, CVE Web Search, Call Chain Analyzer, Function Caller Finder, Function Locator]
Action Input: the specific input for that tool
Observation: the result returned by the tool
... (repeat Thought/Action/Action Input/Observation cycle as many times as needed)
Thought: I now know the final answer
Final Answer: provide the answer with supporting evidence from your investigation
</REACT_FORMAT>

Code Keyword Search(*args, **kwargs) - Performs keyword search on container source code for exact text matches. Input should be a function name, class name, or code pattern. Use this first before semantic search tools for precise lookups.

Function Caller Finder(*args, **kwargs) - Finds functions in a package that call a specific library function. GO ecosystem only.
    Input format: 'package_name,library.function(args_with_literals)'. 
    Example: 'github.com/namespace/package_name,errors.New("text_literal'")'.
    Returns: ['package,caller1', 'package,caller2'] or [].
Function Locator(*args, **kwargs) - Mandatory first step for code path analysis. Validates package names, locates functions using fuzzy matching, and provides ecosystem type (GO/Python/Java/JavaScript/C/C++).
    Make sure the input format is matching exactly one of the following formats:
    
    Input format 1: 'package_name,function_name' or 'package_name,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 1: 'libxml2,xmlParseDocument'.
    
    Input format 2(java): 'maven_gav,class_name.method_name'.
    Example 2(java): 'commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils:1.0.0,PropertyUtilsBean.setSimpleProperty'.
    
    Returns: {'ecosystem': str, 'package_msg': str, 'result': [function_names]}.
</AVAILABLE_TOOLS>

Call Chain Analyzer: Checks if functions are reachable from application code
Function Caller Finder: Finds which functions call specific library functions
Use 'Function Caller Finder' + 'Call Chain Analyzer' together to trace function reachability
CVE Web Search: External vulnerability information lookup
</TOOL_SELECTION_STRATEGY>

{"code_vdb_path": null, "doc_vdb_path": null, "code_index_path": ".cache/am_cache/code_index/tantivy/75237a4bba433f3d", "cve_intel": [{"vuln_id": "CVE-2025-1094", "ghsa": {"ghsa_id": "GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4", "cve_id": "CVE-2025-1094", "summary": "Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(),...", "description": "Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.", "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], "statement": "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.", "cwe_extended_description": null, "configurations": [], "vendor_names": [], "references": ["https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/", "http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3", "http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1", "https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html", "https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html", "https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/"], "disputed": false, "published_at": "2025-02-13T13:15:09.130", "updated_at": "2025-02-21T18:15:20.033"}, "rhsa": {"bugzilla": {"description": "postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation", "id": "2345548", "url": "https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548"}, "details": ["Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.", "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], "statement": "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", code_vdb_path=None doc_vdb_path=None code_index_path='.cache/am_cache/code_index/tantivy/75237a4bba433f3d' cve_intel=[CveIntel(vuln_id='CVE-2025-1094', ghsa=CveIntelGhsa(ghsa_id='GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', cve_id='CVE-2025-1094', summary='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(),...', description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', severity='high', vulnerabilities=[], cvss=CVSS(score=8.1, vector_string='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H'), cwes=[CWE(cwe_id='CWE-149', name='Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax')], published_at='2025-02-13T15:31:25Z', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:31:09Z', url='https://api.github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', html_url='https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', type='unreviewed', repository_advisory_url=None, source_code_location='', identifiers=[{'value': 'GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', 'type': 'GHSA'}, {'value': 'CVE-2025-1094', 'type': 'CVE'}], references=['https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010', 'https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4'], github_reviewed_at=None, nvd_published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09Z', withdrawn_at=None, cvss_severities={'cvss_v3': {'vector_string': 'CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', 'score': 8.1}, 'cvss_v4': {'vector_string': None, 'score': 0.0}}, credits=[], epss={'percentage': 0.80274, 'percentile': 0.99083}), nvd=CveIntelNvd(cve_id='CVE-2025-1094', cve_description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', cvss_vector='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', cvss_base_score=8.1, cvss_severity='HIGH', cwe_name='CWE-149: Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax (4.19.1)', cwe_description='Quotes injected into a product can be used to compromise a system. As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.', cwe_extended_description=None, configurations=[], vendor_names=[], references=['https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/'], disputed=False, published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09.130', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:15:20.033'), rhsa=CveIntelRhsa(bugzilla=Bugzilla(description='postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation', id='2345548', url='https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548'), details=['Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", intel=[CveIntel(vuln_id='CVE-2025-1094', ghsa=CveIntelGhsa(ghsa_id='GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', cve_id='CVE-2025-1094', summary='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(),...', description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', severity='high', vulnerabilities=[], cvss=CVSS(score=8.1, vector_string='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H'), cwes=[CWE(cwe_id='CWE-149', name='Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax')], published_at='2025-02-13T15:31:25Z', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:31:09Z', url='https://api.github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', html_url='https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', type='unreviewed', repository_advisory_url=None, source_code_location='', identifiers=[{'value': 'GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', 'type': 'GHSA'}, {'value': 'CVE-2025-1094', 'type': 'CVE'}], references=['https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010', 'https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4'], github_reviewed_at=None, nvd_published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09Z', withdrawn_at=None, cvss_severities={'cvss_v3': {'vector_string': 'CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', 'score': 8.1}, 'cvss_v4': {'vector_string': None, 'score': 0.0}}, credits=[], epss={'percentage': 0.80274, 'percentile': 0.99083}), nvd=CveIntelNvd(cve_id='CVE-2025-1094', cve_description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.', cwe_extended_description=None, configurations=[], vendor_names=[], references=['https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/'], disputed=False, published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09.130', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:15:20.033'), rhsa=CveIntelRhsa(bugzilla=Bugzilla(description='postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation', id='2345548', url='https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548'), details=['Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", [{"content": "<TASK>
Summarize CVE exploitability investigation results into a clear, evidence-based 
paragraph. The investigation results consist of checklist items (questions) and

Python 3.10.0 is installed (vulnerable version), the urllib.parse module is never 
imported or called in the application codebase (verified via code search). Additionally, 
code analysis revealed that the application does not accept URL inputs from untrusted

files. The combination of no urllib.parse usage and lack of external URL input eliminates 
the attack vector described in the CVE.

- Checklist Item 2: Does the application use the result of PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), or PQescapeStringConn() to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal?
  - Answer: The application does use the result of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], 'statement': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", PQescapeLiteral(),...', description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', severity='high', vulnerabilities=[], cvss=CVSS(score=8.1, vector_string='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H'), cwes=[CWE(cwe_id='CWE-149', name='Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax')], published_at='2025-02-13T15:31:25Z', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:31:09Z', url='https://api.github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', html_url='https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', type='unreviewed', repository_advisory_url=None, source_code_location='', identifiers=[{'value': 'GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', 'type': 'GHSA'}, {'value': 'CVE-2025-1094', 'type': 'CVE'}], references=['https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010', 'https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4'], github_reviewed_at=None, nvd_published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09Z', withdrawn_at=None, cvss_severities={'cvss_v3': {'vector_string': 'CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', 'score': 8.1}, 'cvss_v4': {'vector_string': None, 'score': 0.0}}, credits=[], epss={'percentage': 0.80274, 'percentile': 0.99083}), nvd=CveIntelNvd(cve_id='CVE-2025-1094', cve_description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', cvss_vector='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', cvss_base_score=8.1, cvss_severity='HIGH', cwe_name='CWE-149: Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax (4.19.1)', cwe_description='Quotes injected into a product can be used to compromise a system. As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.', cwe_extended_description=None, configurations=[], vendor_names=[], references=['https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/'], disputed=False, published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09.130', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:15:20.033'), rhsa=CveIntelRhsa(bugzilla=Bugzilla(description='postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation', id='2345548', url='https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548'), details=['Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", [{"content": "<TASK>
Review the CVE exploitability investigation summary and perform two tasks:
1. Classify the exploitability status into one of 12 predefined categories
2. Provide reasoning for your classification, citing specific evidence from the investigation summary
</TASK>

<INSTRUCTIONS>
Select the single most appropriate category based on the investigation findings.

CLASSIFICATION CATEGORIES (in logical precedence order):

1. false_positive - CVE-to-package association is incorrect (wrong package or mismatched CVE)

2. code_not_present - Vulnerable code/library is absent from the container
   (If code is not present, subsequent factors are irrelevant)

3. code_not_reachable - Vulnerable code exists but is never executed at runtime
   (Only applicable if code IS present but execution path analysis shows no calls)

4. requires_configuration - Exploitation requires specific configuration that is disabled
   (Configuration prevents exploitation)

5. requires_dependency - Exploitation requires a dependency that is missing

6. requires_environment - Exploitation requires specific environment that is absent

7. compiler_protected - Compiler flags prevent exploitation

8. runtime_protected - Runtime mechanisms (ASLR, DEP, sandboxing) prevent exploitation

9. perimeter_protected - Network/physical/logical perimeter defenses block exploitation

10. mitigating_control_protected - Other security controls reduce risk

11. uncertain - Insufficient information to determine exploitability

12. vulnerable - Package is actually vulnerable and needs patching

EXPLOITATION CONDITIONS:
For a CVE to be classified as "vulnerable", ALL of these must be true:
- Vulnerable code is PRESENT in the container
- Vulnerable code is USED/CALLED by the application
- Vulnerable code is REACHABLE from attack surfaces (user input, network, file processing)
- No effective mitigations or protections are in place

IF EXPLOITATION CONDITIONS ARE NOT MET:
Select the PRIMARY reason for non-exploitability following the logical precedence 
order above. For example:
- If code is not present: "code_not_present" (even if other factors would also prevent it)
- If code is not reachable: "code_not_reachable" (not "requires_environment")
- If missing dependency prevents it: "requires_dependency"

The categories are ordered by logical precedence. Work through the list from top 
to bottom and select the first category that applies to the situation.

Use "uncertain" only as a final fallback when the investigation truly lacks 
sufficient information to make any determination.
</INSTRUCTIONS>

<OUTPUT_FORMAT>
Provide exactly two lines:

Line 1: category_name (exact category name from the list above)
Line 2: reasoning (brief explanation citing specific evidence from the summary)

Do not include labels like "Category:" or "Reasoning:". Just the values on separate lines.
</OUTPUT_FORMAT>

<EXAMPLE>
code_not_reachable
The vulnerable PIL.ImageMath.eval function exists in the installed Pillow library, but call chain analysis confirmed it is never invoked from application code. The application only uses PIL.Image.open() and PIL.Image.thumbnail() functions, which do not call ImageMath.eval.
</EXAMPLE>

<INVESTIGATION_SUMMARY>
The CVE is exploitable in this container. Investigation confirmed that the PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() functions from the PostgreSQL libpq library are called in the codebase, specifically in source files such as fe-exec.c, common.c, and libpq-fe.h. Furthermore, the application uses the result of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal, as evidenced by Code Keyword Search results and Call Chain Analyzer results showing reachability from the application code. Although the application appears to properly handle and validate input data to prevent malicious SQL injection attacks when using the affected PostgreSQL libpq functions, the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql still presents a potential attack vector.
</INVESTIGATION_SUMMARY>

Provide your justification classification and reasoning on two separate lines:", "additional_kwargs": {}, "response_metadata": {}, "type": "human", "name": null, "id": null, "example": false}]

{'code_vdb_path': None, 'doc_vdb_path': None, 'code_index_path': '.cache/am_cache/code_index/tantivy/75237a4bba433f3d', 'cve_intel': [{'vuln_id': 'CVE-2025-1094', 'ghsa': {'ghsa_id': 'GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', 'cve_id': 'CVE-2025-1094', 'summary': 'Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(),...', 'description': 'Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], 'statement': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.', 'cwe_extended_description': None, 'configurations': [], 'vendor_names': [], 'references': ['https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/'], 'disputed': False, 'published_at': '2025-02-13T13:15:09.130', 'updated_at': '2025-02-21T18:15:20.033'}, 'rhsa': {'bugzilla': {'description': 'postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation', 'id': '2345548', 'url': 'https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548'}, 'details': ['Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], 'statement': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", Furthermore, the application uses the result of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal, as evidenced by Code Keyword Search results and Call Chain Analyzer results showing reachability from the application code. Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', severity='high', vulnerabilities=[], cvss=CVSS(score=8.1, vector_string='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H'), cwes=[CWE(cwe_id='CWE-149', name='Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax')], published_at='2025-02-13T15:31:25Z', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:31:09Z', url='https://api.github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', html_url='https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', type='unreviewed', repository_advisory_url=None, source_code_location='', identifiers=[{'value': 'GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', 'type': 'GHSA'}, {'value': 'CVE-2025-1094', 'type': 'CVE'}], references=['https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010', 'https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4'], github_reviewed_at=None, nvd_published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09Z', withdrawn_at=None, cvss_severities={'cvss_v3': {'vector_string': 'CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', 'score': 8.1}, 'cvss_v4': {'vector_string': None, 'score': 0.0}}, credits=[], epss={'percentage': 0.80274, 'percentile': 0.99083}), nvd=CveIntelNvd(cve_id='CVE-2025-1094', cve_description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.', cwe_extended_description=None, configurations=[], vendor_names=[], references=['https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/'], disputed=False, published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09.130', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:15:20.033'), rhsa=CveIntelRhsa(bugzilla=Bugzilla(description='postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation', id='2345548', url='https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548'), details=['Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", PQescapeLiteral(),...', description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", The Call Chain Analyzer results show that PQescapeLiteral() is reachable from the application code, specifically from the process_psqlrc_file() function in the startup.c file. The Function Caller Finder results show that there are no functions in the package that call PQescapeLiteral(), but the Code Keyword Search results show that PQescapeLiteral() is used in several places in the container source code, including in the psql command-line tool. Therefore, the application does use the result of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql.

Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], 'statement': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.', 'cwe_extended_description': None, 'configurations': [], 'vendor_names': [], 'references': ['https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/'], 'disputed': False, 'published_at': '2025-02-13T13:15:09.130', 'updated_at': '2025-02-21T18:15:20.033'}, 'rhsa': {'bugzilla': {'description': 'postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation', 'id': '2345548', 'url': 'https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548'}, 'details': ['Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], 'statement': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", PQescapeLiteral(),...', description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', severity='high', vulnerabilities=[], cvss=CVSS(score=8.1, vector_string='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H'), cwes=[CWE(cwe_id='CWE-149', name='Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax')], published_at='2025-02-13T15:31:25Z', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:31:09Z', url='https://api.github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', html_url='https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', type='unreviewed', repository_advisory_url=None, source_code_location='', identifiers=[{'value': 'GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', 'type': 'GHSA'}, {'value': 'CVE-2025-1094', 'type': 'CVE'}], references=['https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010', 'https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4'], github_reviewed_at=None, nvd_published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09Z', withdrawn_at=None, cvss_severities={'cvss_v3': {'vector_string': 'CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', 'score': 8.1}, 'cvss_v4': {'vector_string': None, 'score': 0.0}}, credits=[], epss={'percentage': 0.80274, 'percentile': 0.99083}), nvd=CveIntelNvd(cve_id='CVE-2025-1094', cve_description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.
