Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', cvss_vector='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', cvss_base_score=8.1, cvss_severity='HIGH', cwe_name='CWE-149: Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax (4.19.1)', cwe_description='Quotes injected into a product can be used to compromise a system. As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.', cwe_extended_description=None, configurations=[], vendor_names=[], references=['https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/'], disputed=False, published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09.130', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:15:20.033'), rhsa=CveIntelRhsa(bugzilla=Bugzilla(description='postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation', id='2345548', url='https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548'), details=['Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.'}, {'category': 'summary', 'text': 'Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(),...', 'title': 'Vulnerability summary'}, {'category': 'general', 'text': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", 'title': 'Red Hat Security Advisory Statement'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Summary', 'text': 'The CVE is exploitable in this container. Furthermore, the application uses the result of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal, as evidenced by Code Keyword Search results and Call Chain Analyzer results showing reachability from the application code. Although the application appears to properly handle and validate input data to prevent malicious SQL injection attacks when using the affected PostgreSQL libpq functions, the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql still presents a potential attack vector.'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Justification Reasoning', 'text': 'The investigation confirmed that the vulnerable PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() functions from the PostgreSQL libpq library are called in the codebase, are reachable from the application code, and present a potential attack vector through the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, despite proper input handling and validation.'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Justification Label', 'text': 'vulnerable'}], 'product_status': {'known_affected': ['CSAFPID_0001']}, 'threats': [{'category': 'impact', 'details': 'Important', 'date': '2026-03-08T08:36:57Z', 'product_ids': ['CSAFPID_0001']}], 'remediations': [{'category': 'vendor_fix', 'details': 'No remediation recommendation available for this vulnerability.', 'product_ids': ['CSAFPID_0001']}]}]} current_vuln_id=None

Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], 'statement': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.'}, {'category': 'summary', 'text': 'Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(),...', 'title': 'Vulnerability summary'}, {'category': 'general', 'text': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", 'title': 'Red Hat Security Advisory Statement'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Summary', 'text': 'The CVE is exploitable in this container. Investigation confirmed that the PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() functions from the PostgreSQL libpq library are called in the codebase, specifically in source files such as fe-exec.c, common.c, and libpq-fe.h. Furthermore, the application uses the result of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal, as evidenced by Code Keyword Search results and Call Chain Analyzer results showing reachability from the application code. Although the application appears to properly handle and validate input data to prevent malicious SQL injection attacks when using the affected PostgreSQL libpq functions, the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql still presents a potential attack vector.'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Justification Reasoning', 'text': 'The investigation confirmed that the vulnerable PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() functions from the PostgreSQL libpq library are called in the codebase, are reachable from the application code, and present a potential attack vector through the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, despite proper input handling and validation.'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Justification Label', 'text': 'vulnerable'}], 'product_status': {'known_affected': ['CSAFPID_0001']}, 'threats': [{'category': 'impact', 'details': 'Important', 'date': '2026-03-08T08:36:57Z', 'product_ids': ['CSAFPID_0001']}], 'remediations': [{'category': 'vendor_fix', 'details': 'No remediation recommendation available for this vulnerability.', 'product_ids': ['CSAFPID_0001']}]}]}, 'current_vuln_id': None}

PQescapeLiteral(),...', description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', severity='high', vulnerabilities=[], cvss=CVSS(score=8.1, vector_string='CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H'), cwes=[CWE(cwe_id='CWE-149', name='Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax')], published_at='2025-02-13T15:31:25Z', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:31:09Z', url='https://api.github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', html_url='https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', type='unreviewed', repository_advisory_url=None, source_code_location='', identifiers=[{'value': 'GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4', 'type': 'GHSA'}, {'value': 'CVE-2025-1094', 'type': 'CVE'}], references=['https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010', 'https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mhw9-x46c-v6q4'], github_reviewed_at=None, nvd_published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09Z', withdrawn_at=None, cvss_severities={'cvss_v3': {'vector_string': 'CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H', 'score': 8.1}, 'cvss_v4': {'vector_string': None, 'score': 0.0}}, credits=[], epss={'percentage': 0.80274, 'percentile': 0.99083}), nvd=CveIntelNvd(cve_id='CVE-2025-1094', cve_description='Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.', cwe_extended_description=None, configurations=[], vendor_names=[], references=['https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3', 'http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html', 'https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html', 'https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/'], disputed=False, published_at='2025-02-13T13:15:09.130', updated_at='2025-02-21T18:15:20.033'), rhsa=CveIntelRhsa(bugzilla=Bugzilla(description='postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation', id='2345548', url='https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548'), details=['Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.', "A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."], statement="This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.'}, {'category': 'summary', 'text': 'Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(),...', 'title': 'Vulnerability summary'}, {'category': 'general', 'text': "This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.
Even when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.", 'title': 'Red Hat Security Advisory Statement'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Summary', 'text': 'The CVE is exploitable in this container. Investigation confirmed that the PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() functions from the PostgreSQL libpq library are called in the codebase, specifically in source files such as fe-exec.c, common.c, and libpq-fe.h. Furthermore, the application uses the result of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal, as evidenced by Code Keyword Search results and Call Chain Analyzer results showing reachability from the application code. Although the application appears to properly handle and validate input data to prevent malicious SQL injection attacks when using the affected PostgreSQL libpq functions, the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql still presents a potential attack vector.'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Justification Reasoning', 'text': 'The investigation confirmed that the vulnerable PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() functions from the PostgreSQL libpq library are called in the codebase, are reachable from the application code, and present a potential attack vector through the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, despite proper input handling and validation.'}, {'category': 'other', 'title': 'ExploitIQ Analysis Justification Label', 'text': 'vulnerable'}], 'product_status': {'known_affected': ['CSAFPID_0001']}, 'threats': [{'category': 'impact', 'details': 'Important', 'date': '2026-03-08T08:36:57Z', 'product_ids': ['CSAFPID_0001']}], 'remediations': [{'category': 'vendor_fix', 'details': 'No remediation recommendation available for this vulnerability.', 'product_ids': ['CSAFPID_0001']}]}]} current_vuln_id=None

Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.","cwe_extended_description":null,"configurations":[],"vendor_names":[],"references":["https://www.postgresql.org/support/security/CVE-2025-1094/","http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/16/3","http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2025/02/20/1","https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00015.html","https://lists.debian.org/debian-lts-announce/2025/02/msg00024.html","https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20250221-0010/"],"disputed":false,"published_at":"2025-02-13T13:15:09.130","updated_at":"2025-02-21T18:15:20.033"},"rhsa":{"bugzilla":{"description":"postgresql: PostgreSQL quoting APIs miss neutralizing quoting syntax in text that fails encoding validation","id":"2345548","url":"https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2345548"},"details":["Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() allows a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns.  Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected.","A flaw was found in PostgreSQL. Due to improper neutralization of quoting syntax, affected versions potentially allow a database input provider to achieve SQL injection in certain usage patterns. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the affected function's result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal. Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when `client_encoding` is `BIG5` and `server_encoding` is one of `EUC_TW` or `MULE_INTERNAL`."],"statement":"This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.\nEven when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \\! <OS command>) or inject malicious The Call Chain Analyzer results show that PQescapeLiteral() is reachable from the application code, specifically from the process_psqlrc_file() function in the startup.c file. Specifically, SQL injection requires the application to use the function result to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal.  Similarly, improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL command line utility programs allows a source of command line arguments to achieve SQL injection when client_encoding is BIG5 and server_encoding is one of EUC_TW or MULE_INTERNAL.  Versions before PostgreSQL 17.3, 16.7, 15.11, 14.16, and 13.19 are affected."},{"category":"summary","text":"Improper neutralization of quoting syntax in PostgreSQL libpq functions PQescapeLiteral(),...","title":"Vulnerability summary"},{"category":"general","text":"This vulnerability is marked as Important because it not only enables SQL injection but also allows arbitrary code execution (ACE) through PostgreSQL’s psql interactive tool. The flaw arises from improper neutralization of malformed UTF-8 sequences, which can bypass PostgreSQL’s escaping functions, leading to successful injection attacks. What elevates its severity is psql's support for meta-commands, specifically the ! command, which allows execution of arbitrary shell commands on the host system.\nEven when untrusted input is correctly escaped (e.g., via pg_escape_string), an attacker can craft invalid UTF-8 characters containing raw bytes like 0x27 (a single quote), causing psql to misinterpret the SQL statement’s structure. This allows splitting a single query into multiple statements, enabling unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL meta-commands (e.g., \\! <OS command>) or inject malicious SQL.","title":"Red Hat Security Advisory Statement"},{"category":"other","title":"ExploitIQ Analysis Summary","text":"The CVE is exploitable in this container. Investigation confirmed that the PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() functions from the PostgreSQL libpq library are called in the codebase, specifically in source files such as fe-exec.c, common.c, and libpq-fe.h. Furthermore, the application uses the result of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, the PostgreSQL interactive terminal, as evidenced by Code Keyword Search results and Call Chain Analyzer results showing reachability from the application code. Although the application appears to properly handle and validate input data to prevent malicious SQL injection attacks when using the affected PostgreSQL libpq functions, the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql still presents a potential attack vector."},{"category":"other","title":"ExploitIQ Analysis Justification Reasoning","text":"The investigation confirmed that the vulnerable PQescapeLiteral(), PQescapeIdentifier(), PQescapeString(), and PQescapeStringConn() functions from the PostgreSQL libpq library are called in the codebase, are reachable from the application code, and present a potential attack vector through the use of PQescapeLiteral() to construct input to psql, despite proper input handling and validation."},{"category":"other","title":"ExploitIQ Analysis Justification Label","text":"vulnerable"}],"product_status":{"known_affected":["CSAFPID_0001"]},"threats":[{"category":"impact","details":"Important","date":"2026-03-08T08:36:57Z","product_ids":["CSAFPID_0001"]}],"remediations":[{"category":"vendor_fix","details":"No remediation recommendation available for this vulnerability.","product_ids":["CSAFPID_0001"]}]}]}}}
